items.q: What is the difference between physical and economic water scarcity?
items.correct: Physical scarcity is insufficient water supply for demand (e.g. arid regions); economic scarcity is inability to access available water due to lack of infrastructure or investment, despite water being present.
items.misconception: Water scarcity only happens in dry countries.
items.random_wrong: Economic scarcity means water is too expensive for governments to buy.
items.why: Sub-Saharan Africa has economic scarcity — there's water but no pipes, pumps or treatment. Students focus on rainfall; the geographical insight is that scarcity is partly an infrastructure/governance issue.

items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.q: Why do earthquakes typically cause more deaths in LICs than HICs?
items.correct: LICs often have poorly enforced building codes, weak emergency response, dense informal housing and limited preparedness — not because the earthquakes are stronger.
items.misconception: LICs have more earthquake deaths because more earthquakes happen there.
items.random_wrong: HICs never have earthquake deaths because of advanced technology.
items.why: Vulnerability is socio-economic, not seismic. Japan and Haiti both sit on plate boundaries; the death toll difference comes from building quality, planning and response — a key development comparison.

items.q: What is the Hubbert curve about?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peak oil. “Population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's South America. “Africa” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What % of world lithium is in the Lithium Triangle?
items.why: The real figure is 60 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
